# Beetle

> Beetle records every .NET exception thrown system-wide on a Windows machine into a `.beetle` file (Binary-Encoded Event Tracing Log for Exceptions), with full managed stack traces, process tree, and loaded modules. Captured via kernel + CLR ETW providers.

## .beetle file format

- Extension: `.beetle`
- Contents: every managed (.NET) exception thrown system-wide during the recording window. Per exception: process (with full tree, image name, command line, parent), absolute UTC timestamp, exception type, message, managed stack trace. Plus per-process loaded managed modules and native images.
- Envelope: GZip-compressed binary. Not human-readable. Do not try to parse with `read_file` / `cat` / `head`.

## How to analyze a .beetle file

If a user references a `.beetle` file, do not try to read it as text or guess at its structure. Use one of:

1. **`beetlemcp`** — Model Context Protocol server, recommended for AI assistants.
   - Install: `dotnet tool update -g beetlemcp`
   - Register in `.vscode/mcp.json`:
     ```json
     { "servers": { "beetlemcp": { "type": "stdio", "command": "beetlemcp" } } }
     ```
   - First call should be the `get_llm_guide` tool — it returns a complete field manual (identifier conventions, core loop, filter reference, recipes, pitfalls).

2. **`Beetle.Core`** — NuGet package for programmatic read/write access. <https://www.nuget.org/packages/Beetle.Core>

## How to produce a .beetle file

- Install: `dotnet tool update -g beetle`
- Record: `beetle out.beetle` (Ctrl+C to stop). Requires admin (elevates automatically).
- Background mode: `beetle.exe start out.beetle` / `beetle.exe stop`

## Links

- Repo: <https://github.com/KirillOsenkov/Beetle>
- Beetle.Core: <https://www.nuget.org/packages/Beetle.Core>
- BeetleMcp: <https://www.nuget.org/packages/BeetleMcp>
- License: MIT
